problem_summary: 2020 MCM/ICM Problem F (policy type). Tasked with helping the UN address sea-level-rise-driven displacement: (1) measure the severity of the problem (how much land/population is affected, which island nations sink and when); (2) decide which countries should accept the Environmentally Displaced Persons (EDPs) and how the burden is shared; (3-4) model how the unique culture of displaced nations will survive (or erode) after relocation; (5) design policy. This paper (team 2009139) builds a three-part pipeline: a geometric circular-cone model for sinking territory/population, a game-theory + responsibility-index model for country selection, and a nonlinear ODE culture-inheritance model analyzed via Lyapunov stability to drive policy.
models_used: Circular-cone geography model: each coastal country abstracted as a cone parameterized by average altitude H and territory area S; bottom radius R, generatrix L, and apex angle A derived by elementary geometry/calculus (Eq. 1)

Linear population-density model on the cone: rho = k*l (density grows linearly with radius/distance from peak), with k calibrated by integration against World-Bank data on population living below 5 m elevation (Eqs. 2-4)

Sea-level-rise kinematic model: two scenarios — constant speed v0 = 3.8 mm/yr, and accelerated h_y = v0*y + (a/2)*y^2 with a ~= 0.01 mm/yr^2 from 2002-2014 trend; height-to-submerged-area mapping d_y = h_y*L/H (Eqs. 6-7)

Responsibility Index R_country: weighted normalized linear composite of three max-type indicators — accumulated carbon emissions (w=0.5), GDP (w=0.3), population density (w=0.2) (Eq. 8); used to rank ~200 countries and split into Group A (accept EDPs + aid) vs Group B (aid only)

Game-theory negotiation model among 8 great powers: each player picks a 0-1 decision vector x_i=(accept refugees, give aid) over 4 options; 'international pressure' matrix P_t = [p_ij] drives iterative best-response; pressure computed from difference vector XD (reality vs each country's expectation XE), amplified by a CUBIC function and weighted by a favor/hate coefficient matrix FH+/FH-; terminates when >=1 country accepts and all 8 give aid

National Culture Changing model: nonlinear ODE system for three population classes A (assimilated/aborigines), I (full culture-bearers), M (mixed/partial), driven by intermarriage probability p, cultural-inheritance probability alpha, and birth/death rates k_b,k_d (Eq. 12)

Lyapunov second method (direct method): constructed Lyapunov function f(M,I)=a(M-M*)^2+b(I-I*)^2+c(M-M*)(I-I*) to prove stability of the nonzero equilibrium (M*,I*) and derive the analytic policy conditions p < k_d/k_b and alpha >= 2k_d/(k_b*p) - 1 under which culture converges to a stable surviving level rather than vanishing (Eqs. 13-14)
key_techniques: Geometric idealization as a modeling shortcut: replacing real coastline/terrain (intractable) with a cone defined by just two observable national statistics (H, S), making every coastal country computable from public data

Data-driven calibration of an assumed functional form: the linear density law is not left arbitrary — k is pinned by integrating to match the World-Bank 'population below 5 m' figure, converting an assumption into a fitted parameter
Scenario bracketing: constant vs accelerating sea-level rise gives an explicit lower/upper bound on severity instead of a single point prediction

Turning a discrete political bargaining problem into an iterative dynamical system: 'international pressure' as a numerical force that pushes countries stepwise along a preference ordering until an end condition is met — sidesteps the 4^8 = 65536 combinatorial solution space that pure game theory can't enumerate

Encoding politics quantitatively: expectation vectors XE per country, a difference-to-pressure CUBIC nonlinearity (so large disagreement disproportionately raises pressure), and an asymmetric favor/hate matrix (FH+/FH-) to represent alliances
Measuring an abstract concept (culture) by a concrete proxy: 'number of people who master the culture', making culture survival a quantifiable ODE state
Using Lyapunov's second/direct method to get analytic stability thresholds for an unsolvable nonlinear ODE, then reading those inequalities directly as policy levers (keep p small, alpha large)

Single-policy -> combined-policy synthesis: mapping 5 candidate policies onto their effect on (p, alpha), recognizing no single policy dominates, then proposing a TWO-PHASE policy (inhabit+education early, then scatter+strong-protection) to dodge both discrimination and nationalism
Sensitivity analysis via partial derivatives (closed-form dS/dv0, d2S/dv0^2) plus parameter sweeps on weights and on (p, alpha) to argue robustness
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins because it gives a COMPLETE, end-to-end, decision-ready answer to a vague open-ended policy prompt: it produces concrete deliverables a UN reader can act on — a dated sinking timetable for 11 named countries (Tuvalu 2030 ... Samoa 2054), a specific country-pairing schedule (Australia<-Tuvalu, USA<-Maldives, Canada<-Tonga, China<-Marshall, Russia<-Samoa), and a phased culture-protection policy with mathematical justification. Each of the three sub-models is a genuinely different and appropriate tool (geometry+calculus, game theory, ODE+Lyapunov), demonstrating modeling range rather than one hammer. Crucially it does the hard, distinctive thing other teams shy away from: it QUANTIFIES the political/negotiation dimension (the international-pressure game) and the cultural dimension (the inheritance ODE), turning soft policy questions into numbers — exactly what a 'policy' ICM problem rewards. The models are grounded in real data (sea-level rates, World Bank below-5 m population, carbon/GDP/density) and cite domain literature (refugee/environmental law, game-theory-of-politics), giving credibility. It also self-validates (Lyapunov gives an analytic guarantee; sensitivity analysis shows the country ranking is stable to weight changes). The narrative is tightly closed by a 'Exhibition of results' section that explicitly answers all five sub-questions.
section_highlights: Strong structural choices: (1) The Summary front-loads concrete numbers and named outputs (0.16 m by 2050, ~5 million EDPs, the 11 countries, the two-phase policy) so a judge sees the payoff immediately. (2) Standard ICM scaffolding is all present and in order — Introduction, Assumptions (each justified, not just listed), Notations table, then one section per model, then a dedicated 'Exhibition of results' that re-answers every sub-question, sensitivity analysis, and Strengths/Weaknesses. (3) Each model section follows a clean 'simplify reality -> build equations -> calibrate to data -> solve -> tabulate result' rhythm, with figures (cone diagram, submersion diagram, world map of at-risk countries, game flowchart, ODE trend curves) supporting the math. (4) The game-theory section walks through ONE worked iteration in full (showing X, XE_1, XD_1, the pressure vector) before presenting the 16-circle convergence table (Table 7) — this 'show one cycle then show convergence' pattern makes an abstract simulation legible. (5) The culture model explicitly bridges math to policy: the Lyapunov inequalities (small p, large alpha) are translated into named real-world policies in a table, closing the loop from equation to recommendation. Weak spots in the writing: noticeably rough non-native English with typos/garbled fragments throughout (likely OCR/source artifacts), and the equation typesetting has inconsistencies — these did not block the O-award but are not exemplary.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The circular-cone abstraction is geographically crude: real archipelago nations (Maldives, Marshall, Kiribati are flat atolls, not cones) are poorly represented by a single peaked cone; the linear density-decreasing-with-altitude assumption is a strong simplification the authors acknowledge only partially

The game-theory model is essentially a hand-tuned simulation, not a solved game: the international-pressure matrix P_t, the expectation vectors XE_i, and the favor/hate matrix FH+/FH- are set by the modelers' judgment of international politics, so the 'result' (e.g., Australia accepts Tuvalu) reflects assumed inputs as much as model logic — low reproducibility, hard to validate, and the authors admit they 'cannot predict every country's future action'
The cubic difference-to-pressure relation and the upper-pressure-bound of 8.0 are arbitrary choices with no empirical grounding

The culture ODE's key parameters p (intermarriage probability) and alpha (inheritance probability) are not estimated from data; the paper admits it cannot predict how much a given policy actually shifts p or alpha, so the policy mapping (policy -> p,alpha arrows in Table 10) is qualitative/assumed

Sensitivity analysis is thin: it only varies the responsibility-index weights and the (p,alpha) pair; it does NOT test sensitivity to the cone assumption itself, the FH/XE political inputs, or the 30%/50%/80%/100% migration thresholds — the most fragile assumptions go unstressed

Timescales are inconsistent with the policy horizon: the culture model runs out to 700 years and converges over centuries, while the displacement schedule is 2030-2054 — the relevance of an asymptotic equilibrium to near-term policy is asserted, not demonstrated
Some numeric/units sloppiness (e.g., acceleration stated as 0.0925 then '~= 0.01 mm/yr^2', garbled equation fragments) that a stricter review could penalize
Equation (12)/(13) algebra is dense and partly unverified in the text; the Lyapunov coefficient choice (a=b=1, c=2*k_b*p) is presented without motivation
reusable_patterns: Geometric-idealization trick: when real geography/shape is intractable, replace each entity with a simple solid (cone, here) defined by 2-3 publicly available statistics, then derive everything by elementary calculus — lets you apply one model uniformly across ~200 countries/objects

Calibrate an assumed functional form to one anchoring data point: assume a shape (linear density), then fix the free constant by integrating to match a real measured aggregate (World Bank below-5 m population). Converts 'assumption' into 'fitted parameter' and boosts credibility
Scenario-bracketing for predictions: present an optimistic (constant) and pessimistic (accelerating) case to bound an uncertain forecast instead of a single fragile number

Composite responsibility/ability index: build a normalized, max-type, weighted-sum index from a few interpretable indicators to rank candidates and partition them into action tiers (Group A vs B) — generic pattern for any 'who should do what' allocation problem

Iterative best-response simulation to escape combinatorial blow-up: when a discrete decision space is too large for closed-form game solving (4^8), define a scalar 'pressure/force', a preference ordering, and an end condition, then iterate to a fixed point — and SHOW one full iteration plus the convergence table
Proxy-metric for an intangible: quantify a soft quantity (culture, morale, influence) as 'number of people in state X' so it becomes an ODE/dynamical-system state you can model

Lyapunov direct method as both solver and policy generator: for an unsolvable nonlinear ODE, find the equilibrium, build a quadratic Lyapunov function, and read the stability inequalities AS policy levers (here: keep p small, alpha large) — turns a stability proof into actionable recommendations

Single-effect -> combined-strategy policy synthesis: tabulate each candidate policy's effect on the model's key parameters, note that no single option dominates, then design a phased/combined policy that sequences them to avoid each option's downside (here inhabit+education first, scatter+protection later)
Closing 'Exhibition of results' section that explicitly re-answers every numbered sub-question of the prompt — a reliable way to guarantee judges see full coverage
